response:
# Blog Article Page Accessibility Design Plan > **For Claude:** Use a11y-planner protocol. Review with a11y-critic after implementation. > **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA > **Users who need accessibility:** Screen reader users (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver), keyboard-only users, low-vision users (text resize / reflow) > **Assistive technologies:** NVDA (Windows), JAWS (Windows), VoiceOver (macOS), keyboard-only **Feature:** A long-form blog article page (header + nav, main article, author byline with publication date, 4–6 body images, H1/H2/H3 hierarchy, mid-article pull-quote, related-articles sidebar, site footer) rendered by a React/Next.js front-end from Drupal CMS content. **Risk Level:** Low (static reading experience — no interactive widgets, no live regions, no focus traps) **Component Type:** Content page / document (semantic structure, not custom ARIA widgets) --- ## Scope & Context **What:** Accessible semantic structure for a content-heavy article page. This is a reading experience — the accessibility work is correct document semantics, not interaction design. **Why:** Screen reader users navigate long articles by landmarks and headings, not by reading top-to-bottom. A page with no landmark regions and semantically arbitrary heading levels forces them to read linearly with no way to skip the header, jump between sections, or reach the article body. Low-vision users need the text to resize and reflow. The current implementation actively defeats all of this. **Who needs accessibility:** All users, with specific impact on: - Screen reader users — depend on landmarks (`
`, `
`, `
`) and a correct heading outline to navigate. - Keyboard-only users — need a skip link to bypass the header/nav and reach the article. - Low-vision users — need text resize to 200% and reflow at 400% zoom without horizontal scroll. **Compliance target:** WCAG 2.2 AA. **Assistive technologies:** NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, keyboard-only navigation. **Risk level:** Low. No custom widgets, no live regions, no modal/focus-trap. The challenge is completeness and correct semantic element choices, not complex ARIA. **Over-engineering is itself a defect here** — do not add `role` attributes that native elements already provide, and do not invent ARIA widget patterns for static content. **Existing code this modifies (current accessibility state — all defects):** - The existing `Article` component renders raw CMS HTML via `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`. This bypasses React's JSX accessibility linting entirely (eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y cannot inspect a raw HTML string), so no alt-text, heading, or semantics rule fires on the body content. **This is the root cause of the other three defects.** - No landmark regions are defined — the page is a flat stack of `
`s. Violates **WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships** and **2.4.1 Bypass Blocks**. - Images use `alt=""` universally — every image is marked decorative, so informative images are silent to screen readers. Violates **WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content**. - Heading levels are visually styled via design tokens but semantically arbitrary (a visual "H2" may be marked up as an `

` or a styled `
`). The document outline is broken. Violates **WCAG 1.3.1** and **2.4.6 Headings and Labels**. **Constraints (these shape the strategy, not just the checklist):** 1. **Alt text is authored in the CMS by editorial staff with no a11y training.** The component cannot guarantee good alt text — so the plan must define a *fallback contract* for what to render when alt text is missing, empty, or junk, and an editorial guideline for the field. 2. **Heading levels are driven by visual design tokens, not document order.** Visual size must be decoupled from semantic level — the component must map CMS heading data to correct semantic levels independently of the styling token, and never let a designer's font-size choice dictate the `` tag. 3. **The same `Article` component renders on listing pages (teaser) and full article pages.** A teaser must NOT emit an `

` or a `
` landmark — a listing page has many teasers and one page-level `

`. The semantic output must be variant-aware (`teaser` vs `full`). This is the single most likely regression source. --- ## Semantic Structure Plan ### Landmark regions (one of each top-level landmark; native elements preferred) | Region | Element | Notes | WCAG | |--------|---------|-------|------| | Banner | `
` (page-level, not inside `
`) | Site header. Implicit `role="banner"` when a direct child of ``. | 1.3.1 Info and Relationships | | Primary nav | `
` inside `
` | Named because there is more than one `
` on the page. | 1.3.1; 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks | | Main content | `
` | One per page. Skip-link target. Implicit `role="main"`. | 1.3.1; 2.4.1 | | Article | `
` inside `
` | The self-contained article. Implicit `role="article"`. | 1.3.1 | | Sidebar | `
` | Complementary content. Implicit `role="complementary"`. Named to distinguish if multiple asides ever exist and to give the landmark a useful label in the rotor. | 1.3.1 | | Related-articles list nav (optional) | `
` *or* a plain `
    ` inside the `
    ` | If the related items are a navigation block, use a named `
    `; if they are just content links, a `
      ` inside the `
      ` is sufficient. Do not double-label (don't put `aria-label="Related articles"` on both the `
      ` and a nested `
      ` with the same text). | 1.3.1 | | Footer | `
      ` (page-level, direct child of ``) | Site-wide links. Implicit `role="contentinfo"` only when a direct child of `` — if nested, it loses the landmark role. | 1.3.1 | **Disambiguation rule (critical for this fixture):** There are at least two `
      ` landmarks (Primary, and possibly Related articles). When more than one landmark of the same type exists, each must have a distinguishing accessible name via `aria-label` (or `aria-labelledby`). Without it, a screen reader rotor lists "navigation, navigation" with no way to tell them apart. **WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships** (and supports 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks). Do not append the word "navigation" inside the `aria-label` — the role already announces it (avoid "Primary navigation navigation"). ### Heading hierarchy (document outline) Rule — one outline, no skipped levels: - **H1** = the article title. Exactly one `

      ` on a full article page. - **H2** = top-level section headings within the article. - **H3** = sub-section headings nested under an H2. - Never skip a level (no H2 → H4). **WCAG 2.4.6 Headings and Labels** (AA) and **1.3.1 Info and Relationships**. The site header logo/wordmark is **not** the page H1 — the article title is. Sidebar ("Related articles") and footer headings sit *outside* the article's outline; give the sidebar an `

      ` ("Related articles") that is a sibling of the article in page structure, or scope it within the `
      ` — either is acceptable as long as no level is skipped and the article's own `

      ` remains the primary page heading. ### Form structure None. No forms on this page (search, if present in the header, is out of scope per the fixture). ### Skip navigation The first focusable element in the DOM must be a "Skip to main content" link targeting `#main-content`. **WCAG 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks.** Visually hidden until focused (visible on keyboard focus). The target `
      ` must be focusable for the jump to reliably move focus in all browsers — add `tabindex="-1"` to `
      ` (programmatically focusable, not in the tab order). ### Document outline & landmark structure stub (structure only — not implementation code) ``` Skip to main content ← visually-hidden-until-focused
      ← banner [site logo / wordmark — links home]
      … primary menu …
      ← skip-link target

      [Article title]

      By [Author name] · May 15, 2026 ← byline

      … body paragraphs … ← 4–6 body images

      [Section heading]

      [Sub-section heading]

      …
      ← complementary

      Related articles

        … related links …
      ← contentinfo (direct child of body) … site-wide links …
      ``` --- ## Interaction Pattern Design **There are no interactive widgets on this page.** It is a static reading experience. The only interactive elements are plain links (``) — the site logo, primary nav links, in-article links, related-article links, and footer links. These use the native anchor element and need no ARIA pattern, no `role`, and no key handling beyond the browser default (Enter activates a focused link). Per the fixture's guidance, **do not invent APG widget patterns (disclosure, tabs, menu, dialog) for static content.** Adding `role`/`aria-expanded`/arrow-key handling here would be an over-engineering defect. ### Interactive Elements Table | Widget | APG Pattern | Keyboard | ARIA | WCAG | |--------|-------------|----------|------|------| | Skip link | Plain link (no widget pattern) | Tab to focus (first in order); Enter moves focus to `#main-content` | None — native `` | 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks | | Site logo / nav links / in-article links / related links / footer links | Plain link (no widget pattern) | Tab to focus; Enter activates | None — native ``; descriptive link text (see Content plan) | 2.1.1 Keyboard; 2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context) | (No reference to the APG patterns library is needed because no APG *widget* pattern applies. The relevant APG guidance is the [landmark regions practice](https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/practices/landmark-regions/), used in the Semantic Structure Plan above.) --- ## Focus Management Plan Because there are no overlays, modals, drawers, popovers, or dynamically inserted content, focus management here is the **keyboard-only reading path** — not traps or restoration. 1. **Tab order** (matches visual top-to-bottom, left-to-right reading order per **WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order** and **1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence**): 1. Skip-to-main-content link (first focusable element) 2. Site logo (home link) 3. Primary nav links, in source/visual order 4. In-article links, in reading order (interspersed with non-focusable prose) 5. Related-article links in the sidebar 6. Footer links 2. **DOM order = visual order.** Do not reorder content visually with CSS (e.g., fl/grid `order`, absolute positioning) in a way that diverges from DOM order. If the sidebar appears to the right visually but should be read after the article, its DOM position must come after `
      ` (it does in the stub above). **WCAG 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence.** 3. **Skip link target focus.** On activation, focus must land on `
      ` so the next Tab continues from the article, and screen reader reading resumes there. The `tabindex="-1"` makes `
      ` programmatically focusable without inserting it into the tab order. **WCAG 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks; 2.4.3 Focus Order.** 4. **No focus traps, no focus restoration** — nothing opens or closes. (Explicitly noted so a reviewer confirms none was omitted.) 5. **Focus indicators** must be visible on every focusable element (skip link, all links) and meet **3:1** non-text contrast against adjacent colors. **WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible; 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast.** Use `:focus-visible` so the ring shows for keyboard users without appearing on mouse click. Pair `outline` with a `box-shadow` of a contrasting color so the indicator survives on any background (a single-color outline can vanish where it matches the background). Do not remove default outlines without providing a compliant replacement. 6. **No roving tabindex** — there are no composite widgets. --- ## State Communication Design This page has **no interactive state** — nothing expands/collapses, selects, presses, checks, toggles disabled/enabled, or goes busy/idle. There is no application state to communicate to assistive technology, and therefore **no live regions, no `aria-live`, no `role="status"`/`role="alert"`, and no state ARIA attributes** (`aria-expanded`, `aria-selected`, `aria-pressed`, `aria-checked`, `aria-busy`, `aria-invalid`) are used. The only "state" is link visited/unvisited, which the browser and AT handle natively via `:visited` and the link role — no author work required. ### State Communication Table | State | Visual | Programmatic | ARIA | WCAG | |-------|--------|-------------|------|------| | Link focused | Visible focus ring (outline + box-shadow, 3:1) | Native link role + focus | None (native) | 2.4.7 Focus Visible; 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast | | Link visited | `:visited` styling (must not rely on color alone if it conveys meaning) | Native | None (native) | 1.4.1 Use of Color | | (No other states exist) | — | — | — | — | **Note for reviewer:** The empty state table is intentional and correct for a static page. Inventing live regions or state attributes here would be a defect, not a gap. --- ## Visual Accessibility Plan 1. **Color contrast — WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum), AA:** - Body text: **4.5:1** against its background. - Large text (≥ 24px, or ≥ 18.66px bold): **3:1**. Article title and large section headings likely qualify as "large" — confirm per the actual token sizes. - Link text in body copy: must have **3:1** contrast against the surrounding body text color *in addition* to being distinguishable by more than color (see below), and the link text itself must meet 4.5:1 against the page background. - Focus indicators and any non-text UI borders: **3:1** (WCAG 1.4.11). 2. **Color is never the sole indicator — WCAG 1.4.1 Use of Color:** - **In-article links in body prose must be underlined** (or otherwise distinguished by a non-color cue), because color alone cannot distinguish them from surrounding text. Navigation, footer, and obviously-interactive link clusters are exempt, but links embedded in paragraphs are not. - The pull-quote must be distinguishable by structure/position/typography, not color alone. 3. **Font sizing — WCAG 1.4.4 Resize Text:** Use relative units (`rem`/`em`) for all text, never fixed `px` font sizes. Base 16px = 1rem. Text must scale to **200%** without loss of content or function. 4. **Reflow — WCAG 1.4.10 Reflow:** No horizontal scrolling at **400% zoom** (320 CSS px equivalent width). The sidebar must wrap below the article at narrow widths rather than forcing horizontal scroll; the article body must use `max-width: 100%` and relative units. 5. **Line length & spacing — WCAG 1.4.8 Visual Presentation (AAA, recommended for a reading experience) and the 1.4.12 Text Spacing (AA) override test:** - Measure (line length): aim for ~66ch (45–80 characters) for the article body for readability. - Line height ≥ **1.5×** font size; paragraph spacing ≥ **2×** font size; letter spacing ≥ **0.12em**; word spacing ≥ **0.16em** — and the layout must not break when a user applies these via a text-spacing bookmarklet (**WCAG 1.4.12 Text Spacing**, AA). 6. **Reduced motion — WCAG 2.3.3 Animation from Interactions:** Honor `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` (disable any scroll/parallax/reveal animation). Likely minimal here, but include the media-query guard. 7. **Touch target size — WCAG 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum), AA:** Interactive targets (skip link, nav/footer links) at least **24×24** CSS px (2.2 AA minimum), with adequate spacing; aim for 44×44 where layout allows. Inline links within prose are exempt from the minimum. --- ## Content Accessibility Plan ### Image alt text strategy — WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content (the core content task for this fixture) The component receives an alt string and image role from the CMS. Because **editorial staff have no a11y training and the universal `alt=""` is wrong**, the strategy has two parts: a per-image decision tree the editorial field must follow, and a component-level fallback contract. **Decision tree (apply per image):** 1. **Decorative** — the image adds no information (pure visual flourish, divider, background texture, or it merely repeats adjacent text/caption): → render **`alt=""`** (empty, not missing). This makes AT skip it. An *empty* alt is correct; a *missing* alt attribute is a defect (AT may read the filename). 2. **Informative / contextual** — the image conveys information relevant to the article (a photo of the subject, an illustrative scene): → render **concise descriptive alt** (~1 sentence, ≤ ~125 chars), e.g. `alt="Protesters gathered outside the state capitol holding signs"`. Describe the content/function, not "image of"; do not start with "Picture of"/"Image of." 3. **Complex** — chart, diagram, infographic, map, or data figure where a short phrase cannot convey the information: → short `alt` naming the figure **plus** a long description. Provide the long description as visible body text near the figure (best — benefits everyone) or associate it via **`aria-describedby`** pointing at the description element. Use `

_benchmark.method: plan 006 Phase D Claude lane: Agent(subagent_type=a11y-planner, model=opus), one background subagent per fixture; verbatim PLANNER_PROMPT_PREFIX task; agent wrote plan via Write tool; orchestrator wrapped to response JSON
